text: The surgeon adjusts the position to access the anterior leaflet, preparing to excise it.
The surgeon opens up the side of the surgical field, potentially revealing the vagus nerve.
The surgeon pulls up fibers and spreads them laterally to create space, focusing on the angle of the tissue.
The surgeon removes tissue from the left crus, focusing on excising specific structures in this area.

The surgeon inspects the left side for any bleeding, noting no significant hemorrhage. They proceed to take down the short gastric vessels and prepare the area, while planning to open the hiatus and mobilize the esophagus further downward.

The surgeon injects the esophagus with a solution of epinephrine, aiming to reduce bleeding and create a wheal that helps separate the veins. The injection is performed near a small vein visible on the surface.

The surgeon identifies the z-line, a visible anatomical landmark at the gastroesophageal junction, and discusses the need to move slightly further down, approximately another centimeter, to reach the desired location.
